coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus doped carbon dots additive improves the performance of perovskite solar cells via defect passivation in MAPbI3 films
coredata.dc:creator: Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Çisem
Yılmaz, Tuğbahan
Büyükbekar, Alihan
Tok, Mütahire
Kuş, Mahmut
coredata.dc:description: Perovskites have been widely investigated as absorber layer in photovoltaic technology owing to their excellent properties. For further enhancement of the power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells, here, we successfully synthesized phosphorus doped carbon dots (P-CQDs) by solvothermal method and investigated their effect as an additive on the perovskite film properties and photovoltaic performance. The results showed that electron-rich phosphorus can act as an excellent core unit in perovskite absorber layer to passivate defects at the surface for improved the crystallization and charge transport properties. Thanks to passivation effect of P-CQDs on perovskite films, as well as the suitable energy band gap values and optical properties of MAPbI3 layer, the device prepared with the addition of 5 vol% of P-CQDs achieved a champion PCE of 13.12% with a high fill factor (FF) of 73.44%, higher than the PCE of 10.71% with a FF of 58.81% for control device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
Defect passivation
P-CQDs
Additive engineering
Crystallinity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of P-CQDs and thin films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the device fabrication.
Ratios of the XRD intensities of the PbI2 peak and the perovskite (110) peak (red dots) and the maximum intensities of the (110) planes of the perovskite (black dots).
The 2D AFM images of MAPbI3 thin films with (a) 0 vol%, (b) 1 vol%, (c) 3 vol%, and (d) 5 vol% of P-CQDs in perovskite solution.
Tauc plots extracted from absorption curves of MAPbI3 thin films prepared with the addition of different vol% of P-CQDs.
(a) J-V curves of the best performing devices prepared with the addition of different vol% of P-CQDs, (b) EQE spectra of the devices.
Statistical distributions of the J-V measurements of (a) VOC, (b) JSC, (c) FF, and (d) PCE of 20 devices prepared with different vol% of P-CQDs.
The average roughness (Ra), grain size distribution, and crystal size values of perovskite films prepared with different concentration of P-CQDs.
Summary of photovoltaic parameters of PSCs different concentration of P-CQDs with the best PCEs.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus doped carbon dots additive improves the performance of perovskite solar cells via defect passivation in MAPbI3 films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075, Konya, Turkey.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electric and Energy, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electric and Energy, Konya Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electric and Energy, Konya Technical University, 42075, Konya, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perovskites have been widely investigated as absorber layer in photovoltaic technology owing to their excellent properties. For further enhancement of the power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells, here, we successfully synthesized phosphorus doped carbon dots (P-CQDs) by solvothermal method and investigated their effect as an additive on the perovskite film properties and photovoltaic performance. The results showed that electron-rich phosphorus can act as an excellent core unit in perovskite absorber layer to passivate defects at the surface for improved the crystallization and charge transport properties. Thanks to passivation effect of P-CQDs on perovskite films, as well as the suitable energy band gap values and optical properties of MAPbI3 layer, the device prepared with the addition of 5 vol% of P-CQDs achieved a champion PCE of 13.12% with a high fill factor (FF) of 73.44%, higher than the PCE of 10.71% with a FF of 58.81% for control device.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In last decade, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have significantly developed amongst other photovoltaic technologies as perovskite materials present desired optical and electronic properties such as broad absorption spectra, high optical absorption coefficient, tunable band gap, long carrier diffusion distance (∼1 µm) for holes and electrons [1–3]. Due to the certified power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs reached to over 25%, the possibility of developing low-cost large-area devices and solution processability, the researchers focus on this technology [4]. As we know, the photovoltaic performance of PSCs depends on the quality of perovskite absorber films with well-crystallized grains, homogenous morphology, and high surface coverage [5]. In addition to all these, one of the biggest problems to become a competitive alternative of PSCs in photovoltaic market is that the high-performance devices have been fabricated under inert atmosphere to provide long-term stability, which limits the further large-scale production [6].

To improve the performance of PSCs, various approaches including different deposition of perovskite layer [7], anti-solvent treatment [8,9], interfacial engineering [10], additive engineering and surface passivation [11] have been developed. Recently, surface passivation approach has been proven to be the most promising strategy because of ease of application and low cost [12]. For PSCs, surface passivation can be achieved by the addition of grain boundary passivation molecules or nanomaterials into perovskite absorber layer [13], the addition of passivation layer [14], and even surface passivation by oxygen [15]. The addition of passivation materials into perovskite layer is an effective way to address the losses at the grain boundary and interfaces [16]. So far, several materials, such as, inorganic halide based (MgCl2 [17], ZnCl2 [18]), organic solvents [19], fullerenes [20], inorganic acids [21], CsPbBr3 nanocrystals [22], and small molecules [23] have been adopted to control the uniformity of film and to passivate grain boundaries. The working of passivation mechanism of additives is generally explained by the forming of strong ion bonds with uncoordinated Pb2+ between their functional groups and perovskite. Additionally, the additives can create hydrogen bonds with methylammonium (MA) and halide ions, leading to improve the Pb−I bonding and MA−I bonding [24]. With this, ion migration and non-radiative recombination can be reduced, which improves the device stability under atmospheric conditions.

Recent studies showed that quantum dots (QDs) are a good choice to be used as an additive due to their adjustable bandgap by controlling their size, their high photostability, surface functionalization, leading to have improved light-harvesting properties to produce optoelectronic devices [25]. Besides, QDs can be implemented into the solar cell structure as either hole transport layer (HTL) or electron transport layer (ETL) [6]. Especially, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been proven to be efficient material in PSCs configuration for defect diminution, along with improved charged transport properties and fill factor (FF) at the same time [26]. A variety of methods for the preparation of highly luminescent and flexible functionalization CQDs, including doping of hetero-atoms or surface passivation were reported to provide oxygen-containing functional groups abundant quantum dots [27]. The doping of CQDs with atomic impurities (e.g., boron, sulfur, nitrogen, etc.) is one of the main strategies for adjusting their intrinsic properties. Zang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs by solvothermal method to tune optical properties of QDs, and they reported that the amino functionalized showed high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) and excellent stability [28]. Even though the origin for the increase in PL QY has not been illuminated, there are different proposals such as the band gap tuneability via charge transfer effect, the quantum confinement effect, or the excitons emission in surface energy traps and defects by passivation [29,30]. So far, important efforts have been given in developing QDs with improved properties. However, the studies on their potential applications in PSCs are surprisingly very limited. In our previous studies, the addition of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) and boron-doped CQDs (B-CQDs) into methyl ammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3) perovskite active layer were examined, and their effect in solar cell performance were reported [31,32]. In those studies, we prepared highly luminescent N-CQDs and B-CQDs with PL QY over 22.7% and 12.4%, respectively, at large scale. It was revealed that the addition of luminescent hetero-atom doped CQDs into the perovskite layer improves the charge transport properties of PSCs and their stability. Differently, phosphorus (P) shows high reactivity since it has electron-rich nature. Therefore, the introducing P into CQDs will bring novel optical features [33]. For instance, P-CQDs synthesized showed different emission results depending on the excitation conditions [34]. To date, much research have been reported to prepare highly luminescent P-CQDs [35,36]. In this study, we synthesized highly luminescent P-CQDs (PL QY of 27.8%) with optimized dosing of P heteroatom. However, to the best of our knowledge, the studies on the effect of phosphorus doping as hetero-atom to CQDs (P-CQDs) for the fabrication of high-performance PSCs is not performed. According to the literature, an electron rich system can be obtained in MAPbI3 perovskite layer by the addition of P-CQDs owing to the doping with the electron-rich phosphorus acting as an excellent core unit [36,37]. Additionally, phosphorus behaves like an n-type donor forming substitutional defects, unlike nitrogen acting as an acceptor [38,39]. Thus, phosphorus can provide more active sites on the CQDs surface. Therefore, in this study, P-CQDs synthesized by solvothermal method are proposed to be utilized as novel additive in p-i-n structured PSCs. The optical, electrical, and surface properties, and crystallization of the perovskite film were promoted by the addition of P-CQDs by the passivation effect. Furthermore, the optimum concentration of P-CQDs additive was determined, which was presented that the charge collection properties, increasing the FF of a PSCs can be well controlled by addition 5 vol% of P-CQDs. As a result, the PCE of best performing PSC were increased from 10.7% to 13.1%. In addition to improved optical properties, the energy bandgaps regulated showed a beneficial impact on the charge extraction and transport properties. More importantly, the reproducibility of devices was increased, which could be related to reduced surface defects showing the efficacy of P-CQDs as an additive for passivation.

Hellmanex III solution, concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4), and other reagents including urea (99%), citric acid (99%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, anhydrous, 99.8%), γ-butyrolactone (GBL, ≥99%), chlorobenzene, toluene, acetone, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) etc were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. ITO substrates (with a sheet resistance of 9–15 Ω sq−1), 2,9-dimethyl-4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline (BCP), [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) were supplied by Lumtec Corp. The commercial aqueous solution of poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) solution (PEDOT:PSS, Clevios™ P VP Al 4083) was bought from Heraeus Group. All materials were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout this work obtained from a Milli-Q Direct-Q® 3 UV water purification system.

The solvothermal method was implemented to synthesize P-CQDs. Firstly, 1.5 g of citric acid, 1.5 g of urea, 5 mL of concentrated H3PO4 solution and 100 mL of DMF were mixed and stirred at room temperature for 20 min. Then, the solution was kept at 180 °C for 24 h in a Teflon-coated autoclave. The resulting suspension turned to a transparent dark brown solution, which indicated the formation of P-CQDs. At last, the product was removed from the solvent by centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 30 min and the residue was washed with ultrapure water. The removing and washing were repeated for three times. The obtained P-CQDs were dried and kept at 4 °C for further use. For the preparation of P-CQDs additive solution, the dried product was dissolved in DMF at concentration of 10 mg mL−1. Before the addition into perovskite solution, the P-CQDs additive solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter to remove the aggregates.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of the PSC device fabrication. At first, the ITO coated glasses (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) were cleaned by diluted Hellmanex solution, deionized water, acetone, isopropanol, and deionized water by ultrasonic bath for 15 min, respectively. Then, cleaned ITO substrates were dried by a nitrogen gun. Then, the oxygen plasma treatment was performed by Diener Plasma System Femto PCCE - Plasma Cleaner for 5 min to enhance the ITO wetting and remove organic residuals. PEDOT:PSS was coated on the ITO substrates as a hole transport layer at 1500 rpm for 15 s, 2000 rpm for 50 s, and 3000 rpm for 2 s and then the coated substrates were annealed at 140 °C for 10 min in air. To prepare perovskite solution, PbI2 (1.23 M) and CH3NH3I (1.54 M) were dissolved in 2.5 mL of GBL, and the solution was stirred at 70 °C for 2 h. Different % vol. of P-CQDs addition (0%, 1%, 3% and 5%) into perovskite solution was studied to improve the PSCs performance. It should be noted that the higher vol. of P-CQDs resulted in a strong aggregation of perovskite solution, which could lead to coat a non-homogenous perovskite layer. Solvent washing method was employed to deposit perovskite layer. The perovskite solution with P-CQS was coated by two steps spin coating at 1000 rpm for 10 s, followed by at 5000 rpm for 30 s and 80 μL toluene was poured to the spinning substrates at last 10 s to form a homogenous perovskite film. The perovskite coated substrates were annealed at 100 °C for 20 min. PCBM solution (20 mg mL−1 in chlorobenzene) was spin coated onto perovskite layer as an electron transport layer at 1500 rpm for 17 s, and at 2000 rpm for 17 s, followed by annealed at 90 °C for 1 min. Subsequently, BCP solution (0.5 mg mL−1 in ethanol) was spin coated at 1000 rpm for 2 s, at 2000 rpm for 2 s, and at 4000 rpm for 40 s. Then, the substrates were annealed at 90 °C for 1 min. Finally, 110 nm silver were thermally evaporated at the pressure under 1.0 × 10−6 Torr using the physical vapor deposition (PVD) technique. The device's active area was determined to be 0.023 cm2.

Absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a Biochrom Libra S22 spectrometer and a Perkin Elmer LS-55 photoluminescent spectrometer, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of P-CQDs were recorded by a JEOL JEM 2100 FHRTEM. Crystalline properties of perovskite thin films were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns using a Bruker Advance D8 X-ray diffractometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to examine the morphology of thin films by a NT-MDT AFM NTEGRA Solaris in “tapping” mode. Surface roughness was calculated as the root-mean-square roughness (Ra) from 10 × 10 µm2 AFM images. The current density-voltage (J–V) characteristics were examined by a Keithley 2400 source meter under a solar simulator (ATLAS) with illumination intensity of 100 mA cm−2, 1.5 AM light source in glove box at ambient temperature. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) was recorded as a function of wavelength by a solar cell quantum efficiency measurement system (Newport QE-PV-SI) with a 300 W xenon lamp as the source light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P-CQDs and thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Successful synthesis of P-CQDs with %2.77 of phosphorus doping was investigated by UV-Vis, PL, and SEM-EDX mapping. UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy were performed to understand optical properties of the P-CQDs. The absorption spectra of P-CQDs and the PL spectra of P-CQDs were shown in Fig. S1 (a) and (b), respectively. A strong absorption peak of P-CQDs at around 270 nm was observed. This peak could be related to n-π * electronic transition of functional groups including CO, P = O, and CP groups [34,40]. The P-CQDs solution gave a strong bluish fluorescence emission under UV-lamp (at around 365 nm), which could be attributed to their small sizes (∼3–6 nm) [41]. In addition, the PL spectra of P-CQDs was recorded as the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 380 nm, and a strong emission peak observed at around 450 nm peak which is correlated to bright blue-light under UV-light. The emission intensity of P-CQDs increased the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 370 nm, then thereafter decreased the excitation wavelength at 380 nm. According to the UV-Vis spectra and PL of P-CQDs, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap for the blue fluorescent in the P-CQDs should be rather substantial [42]. The representative TEM image of P-CQDs was given in Fig. S2, which showed homogenously distributed spherical shaped P-CQDs. A particle size distribution (%) graph was also given in Fig. S2 and the average particle diameter was determined to be 4–6 nm. The average particle diameter showed that the P-CQDs were successfully synthesized as quantum-sized nanomaterials. The amount of phosphorus element in the CQDs was characterized by SEM-EDX mapping technique. The result of SEM-EDX of as prepared P-CQDs indicated that the spectrum of P-CQDs displayed dramatically presence of phosphorus (11.28 norm. wt%), carbon (42.72 norm. wt%), and oxygen (45.99 norm. wt%) as seen in Fig. S3. These results clearly indicated that P-CQDs were successfully synthesized.

To illuminate the origin of photovoltaic performance enhancement in the PSCs prepared with the addition of P-CQDs, the optical, morphological, and structural properties of MAPbI3 thin films were investigated. The Fig. S4 shows the XRD patterns of well-prepared MAPbI3 layer by the addition of P-CQDs compared with the XRD pattern of the bare MAPbI3. The well-defined diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 14.5°, 20.4°, 23.9°, 24.9°, 28.8°, 32.3°, 35.6°, 41.0°, 43.6°, 50.6°, and 60.4° which were corresponded to the MAPbI3 planes (110), (200), (211), (202), (220), (310), (312), (224), and (330), respectively [43,44]. The XRD measurement could well distinguish the outcome of P-CQDs additive due to increasing crystal size. The most intense PbI2 peak located at 2θ value of 13.13° was assigned to the (001). In addition, other peaks observed at 34.2°, and 38.67° were related to the (102), and (003) lattice planes of PbI2, respectively [44]. A negligible quantity of PbI2 residual may enhance the perovskite's performance by passivating defect states, but because to its inherent instability, it will cause the film to degrade in humid environments and under light [45,46]. Furthermore, the intensity ratios of the (110) plane of the perovskite and the signals of PbI2 is presented in Fig. 2. According to this result, the amount of PbI2 in the films with 1 vol%, and 5 vol% addition of P-CQDs were higher than those in the films with 3 vol% addition of P-CQDs. On the other hand, the contents of PbI2 in the film with 0 vol% addition of P-CQDs is not observed. It could be said that adding P-CQDs in the perovskite film leads to enhance the peak intensities of perovskites indicating that the P-CQDs additions improved the perovskite crystallization, resulting in bigger grains. As summarized in Table 1, the crystallize size of perovskite films was measured to be 69.17 nm, 82.89 nm, 71.55 nm, and 61.56 nm for 0 vol%, 1 vol%, 3 vol%, and 5 vol% addition of P-CQDs, respectively, indicating that these results clearly depicted the increased crystallization of perovskite films after the addition of P-CQDs.

In attempt to understand the influence of P-CQDs on the surface morphology of perovskite film layer, the AFM analysis was performed. Fig. 3 depicts the morphology of the well-prepared MAPbI3 layer by the addition of P-CQDs. The morphological examination of Fig. 3 indicated that the average roughness (Ra) values are measured to be 6.268 nm, 9.320 nm, 9.002 nm, and 7.925 nm for 0 vol%, 1 vol%, 3 vol%, and 5 vol% addition of P-CQDs, respectively, as seen in Table 1. The increase in the vol% of P-CQDs additive of the MAPbI3 layer contributed to the cause of the increase in the Ra value. The highest Ra value obtained after 1 vol% addition of P-CQDs additive of the MAPbI3 layer compared to the other surfaces. Increased surface roughness can lead to a larger perovskite crystal size, which is attributed to better light absorption and scattering [47,48]. In addition, the grain size distribution of MAPbI3 layer by the addition of P-CQDs was measured to investigate the process of perovskite crystallization since adding P-CQDs into MAPbI3 layer could attributed to obtaining a strong PbI2 and CO interaction which is attributed to delay rapid the process of perovskite crystallization by means of increasing perovskite grain size [43,48]. Therefore, the grain size distribution of MAPbI3 layer by the addition of P-CQDs was measured by using NOVA program integrated with AFM. The grain size distribution of MAPbI3 layer were summarized in Table 1 which increased from 35.496 nm for 0 vol% addition of P-CQDs to 51.726 nm, 45.710 nm, and 41.371 nm for 1 vol%, 3 vol%, and 5 vol% addition of P-CQDs, respectively. It is decreasing the perovskite films' grain boundaries and carrier recombination while increasing grain size which could lead to a driven charge transport mechanism in perovskite films. Therefore, the photovoltaic parameters of PSCs can be improved. Fig. S5 (a-d) show the top-view SEM images of the perovskite layer prepared by 0, 1, 3, and 5 vol% addition of P-CQDs, respectively. It is obviously seen that all films presented homogenous surface coverage with well-packed crystalline grains (450–750 nm). However, the larger crystalline domains were obtained in comparison to the MAPbI3 layer without P-CQDs additive, which helps to lead better charge extraction and transport [49]. Additionally, the MAPbI3 layer without P-CQDs additive displayed some holes and white spots related to the phase segregation of PbI2. These results support the findings of XRD patterns.

For further understand the effect of P-CQDs additive on the optical and photophysical properties of perovskite thin films, UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded and the results were given in Fig. S6. With the addition of P-CQDs to the perovskite solution, similar absorbance with a slight increase 500–800 nm light-absorption range was observed, which indicated the small improvement in the photo-harvesting capability of perovskite absorber layer. This could be attributed to the better crystallinity and higher grain sizes obtained by the incorporation of P-CQDs, with similar MAPbI3 film thickness [50]. Also, the extracted energy band gap (Eg) values of MAPbI3 films from the Tauc plot were illustrated in Fig. 4. Similar to our study previously reported [31], the addition of P-CQDs into the MAPbI3 resulted a slight increase in the Eg values, in accordance with the UV-Vis absorption spectra. It was revealed that the P-CQDs was not affect the composition of MAPbI3, but provided a blue-shift in absorption region, which could increase the light-absorption of absorber layer [51].

The J-V curves of the best performing devices prepared with/without the addition of P-CQDs were given in Fig. 5(a). The short-circuit current density (JSC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), and FF of these devices were listed in Table 2. The device prepared by the addition of 5 vol% of P-CQDs showed the highest efficiency of 13.12%. With the addition of P-CQDs, the FF values of all samples significantly increased from 58.81% to 73.44%. The increased FF values were possibly due to the decreased PbI2 accumulation at the perovskite surface, promoting better charge extraction between the perovskite and charge transport layers. The functional groups (like hydroxyl groups) on P-CQDs could create hydrogen bonds with iodide negative ions and form nucleation sites, slowing the perovskite crystallization, which allows to modulation of the crystal orientation and minimization the trap-state density [52]. With the passivation of perovskite defects such as iodine vacancy, electron-rich P-CQDs addition also improved charge transport properties [53]. Furthermore, the JSC values were slightly higher than that of control device, except for the device with 3 vol% of P-CQDs additive. It could be attributed to absorbing more photons with the addition of P-CQDs, leading to more electron-hole pairs [54]. The exception for the device with 3 vol% of P-CQDs additive may be due to the aggregation of P-CQDs. The series resistance (Rs) and shunt resistance (Rsh) values can be also used to provide further information on were extracted from J-V curves, and the results were given in Table S1. The Rsh values increased from 1024 Ω cm−2 of control device to 1090 Ω cm−2, 1315 Ω cm−2, and 1405 Ω cm−2 after the addition of increased vol% of P-CQDs, respectively, whereas the Rs values decreased from 19 Ω cm−2 of control device to 13 Ω cm−2, 9 Ω cm−2, and 6 Ω cm−2. These results were in accordance with the device performances.

Moreover, the EQE spectra of the champion devices at different vol% of P-CQDs were recorded (Fig. 5(b)). The EQE spectra represented that the carrier’s collection for the devices with P-CQDs additive were slightly higher than that of the control device. It can be concluded that the P-CQDs addition could suppress the recombination process, as well as improve photo-induced carriers harvesting, relating to decreased charge trap density [50]. The control device showed a decrease over 500 nm. At same region, the devices prepared with 1 and 3 vol% of P-CQDs additive presented a slight increase, while the device prepared with 5 vol% of P-CQDs additive showed a significant improvement. An increase in this region is mainly related to the enhanced charge extraction at perovskite surface owing to recombination [55]. This result was in accordance with the assumption that P-CQDs additive passivates the perovskite defects. Besides, the integrated JSC values from the EQE curves matched well with the JSC obtained from J-V curves (Table 2).

Fig. 6 shows the comparison of the photovoltaic characteristics of 20 devices prepared with different vol% of P-CQDs added in perovskite layers. The average values of FF and PCE after the addition of appropriate vol% of P-CQDs were higher than those of control device by 19.25% for FF (58.22–69.43%) and 12.7% for PCEs (10.89–12.27%). Beside suitable energy band gap values of MAPbI3, the defect passivation of perovskite thin films was important to obtain more reproducible results and suppress charge back recombination, improving the photovoltaic performance [56].

In summary, an additive engineering method was adopted for the doping of perovskite absorber layer. The defect diminution, along with improved charged transport and suppressed charge recombination at the same time were promoted in P-CQDs added MAPbI3 film, at 5 vol% of P-CQDs concentration. The characterization of thin films and the devices presented that electron-rich P-CQDs can passivate the defects, acting as a core unit in MAPbI3 and modulate the perovskite crystallization. Additionally, the additive engineering method can be used to tailor the photophysical characteristics and light-harvesting capability. The addition of appropriate vol% of P-CQDs passivated the grain boundaries, leading to form better surface coverage. The addition of 5 vol% of P-CQDs into perovskite layer yielded in a important enhancement in photovoltaic performance from 10.71% to 13.12%.

Çisem Kırbıyık Kurukavak: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition. Tuğbahan Yılmaz: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Alihan Büyükbekar: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Mütahire Tok: Review & editing, Investigation. Mahmut Kuş: Review & editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank to financial support of the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TÜBİTAK) (PN:121F377), Konya Technical University Scientific Research Council (PN: 221016007), and EngSurf-Twin project (GA: 952289) European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.105668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells based on carbon-counter-electrode via anti-solvent treatment
J. Alloy. Compd.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Chen, H. Bala, S. Yao, B. Zhang, N. Sha, X. An, W. Zhang, D. Chen, Enhanced efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells based on carbon-counter-electrode via anti-solvent treatment, Journal of Alloys and Compounds, 920 (2022) 165874.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct measurement of the exciton binding energy and effective masses for charge carriers in organic–inorganic tri-halide perovskites
Nat. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Miyata, A. Mitioglu, P. Plochocka, O. Portugall, J.T.-W. Wang, S.D. Stranks, H.J. Snaith, R.J.
